Image Description: Baseline output gallery showing the default-parameter results for each of the five scripts. This establishes the reference conditions against which all subsequent parameter-variation experiments are compared.

IP-Adapter uniform scale sweep grid showing generated images across $\lambda \in \{0.0, 0.1, \ldots, 1.0\}$ with multiple seeds per scale value. The grid illustrates the progression from unconditioned generation (left) to high-fidelity reference-guided synthesis (right).

Block-level configuration comparison showing style transfer results for different layer activations within `block_0`, different block selections, and different scale intensities. All outputs use the text prompt "a cat inside of a box" with the Mamoeiro reference image.

Uniform versus block-level IP-Adapter comparison. Uniform scaling ($\lambda = 0.5$) applies reference image influence to both content and style, while block-level scaling selectively transfers style while preserving text-driven content. Same prompt, reference image, and seeds used for both conditions.

Mean WER and CER for four ASR models (wav2vec 2.0, Whisper-tiny, Whisper-small, Whisper-medium) evaluated on 20 MINDS-14 en-AU samples at the correct 16 kHz sampling rate. Error bars indicate standard deviation across samples.

Sampling rate mismatch effect comparing WER at the correct 16 kHz rate versus mismatched 8 kHz rate for wav2vec 2.0 and Whisper-small. Both models exhibit severe WER degradation when audio is provided at an incorrect sampling rate.

Bark TTS generation time scaling across text inputs of varying length (short, medium, long) and domain (conversational, technical, proper-noun). Generation time scales approximately linearly with text length at a ratio of ~2.3× audio duration.

Round-trip WER from Bark TTS generation followed by ASR transcription, evaluated across short, technical, and proper-noun text inputs. Lower WER indicates higher intelligibility of the synthesized speech.

Seed sensitivity summary comparing generation time variability across eight seeds for three generative paradigms: GA18B (uniform-scale image), GA18C (block-level image), and GA19C (Bark TTS). Higher dispersion indicates greater seed sensitivity.

Cross-modal paradigm comparison summarizing mean generation time, number of controllable parameters, stochasticity level, and evaluation approach for IP-Adapter image generation (GA18B/GA18C), ASR (GA19B), and Bark TTS (GA19C).

Cross-modal paradigm comparison summarizing mean generation time, number of controllable parameters, stochasticity level, and evaluation approach for IP-Adapter image generation (GA18B/GA18C), ASR (GA19B), and Bark TTS (GA19C).
